Lear, Lover, or Lunatic
01 Introduction 02 Methodology 03 The Six Types 04 Conclusion 05 References
PBL Module 2 Report · Digital English Linguistics

Lear, Lover,
or Lunatic

Mapping Shakespeare for the Streaming Library
Course디지털영어학입문 · 이종현 교수
Team우재성 (Leader) · 김현아 · 주지현 · 진일명
Submitted2026년 5월 31일
↓ Scroll — 37 plays, 2 dimensions, 6 types
01

Introduction

Why Reclassify Shakespeare?

Our team is part of the content curation team at a global streaming service. The platform wants a new way to organize its library so that viewers can discover content through reference points like “Hamlet-type” or “Twelfth Night-type.” This report is the first step: analyzing Shakespeare’s 37 plays, grouping them into data-supported types, naming each after a representative work, and mapping each to the viewers it suits.

Shakespeare’s plays have been sorted into three buckets for four centuries: comedies, tragedies, and histories. That taxonomy was built for the stage, not for recommendation. It is too coarse for the question a streaming platform faces daily: what should I recommend to this viewer, right now? “Tragedy” covers both the domestic suffocation of Othello and the political collapse of Julius Caesar. “Comedy” bundles the dark irony of Measure for Measure with the breezy warmth of As You Like It.

Genre labels describe what a work is, not what it feels like to watch. The viewing experience is shaped by two things above all: how the emotional register moves across the work, and how the characters are connected. Neither is captured by genre. This project classifies the 37 plays using only those two measurable dimensions.

Research Question

If we set aside inherited genre labels entirely and classify Shakespeare’s 37 plays using only measurable emotional trajectory and character network structure, what patterns emerge — and do those patterns serve streaming curation better than comedy / tragedy / history?

02

Methodology

How the Types Were Derived
Stage 1

Measuring Emotional Flow

We applied VADER sentiment scoring to the dialogue of all 37 plays. Every speech unit received a compound score from −1 to +1. Scores were averaged at the scene level and smoothed using LOWESS regression (bandwidth 200) to reveal the underlying arc.

Why scene-level (not word- or act-level)?

Word-level scoring captures fluctuations too granular to reflect a viewer’s experience. Act-level averaging collapses too much variation — a long act with swings in both directions produces a misleading mid-range average. The scene is the natural dramatic unit at which Shakespeare builds emotional contrast.

Why VADER and LOWESS?

VADER reads valence-bearing word choices without requiring modern grammar — suitable for verse. LOWESS at bandwidth 200 removes scene-to-scene noise while preserving the macro arc. A tighter bandwidth over-fits to local noise; a wider one erases meaningful mid-play shifts such as the central valley in Henry V-type plays.

Stage 2

Measuring Character Relationship Structure

Networks were built from speech co-occurrence: characters sharing a scene form an edge, weighted by co-occurrence frequency. The Leiden algorithm detected communities. Four metrics were recorded: Density, Q Modularity, Communities, and Average Edge Weight.

Why speech co-occurrence?

Direct address misses characters who share scenes without speaking directly; stage directions are inconsistent across editions. Co-occurrence within scenes is the most reliable structural signal of dramatic relationship.

Why Leiden over Louvain?

Leiden guarantees internally connected communities, which Louvain does not. For dramatic analysis, where a community should mean a coherent group of characters, this matters.

Stage 3

How the Number of Types Was Determined

The six types were not chosen in advance. They emerged from the intersection of two axes — emotional arc shape and network structure — applied to all 37 plays.

The first axis sorted plays by the geometric shape of their LOWESS-smoothed sentiment curve, producing five distinct forms: continuous descent, continuous rise, mid-play valley (down-then-up), mid-play arch (up-then-down), and flat plateau.

Within the descending-arc group, a critical sub-split appeared. Some plays cross into genuinely negative sentiment in their final acts (negative-entry status), while others descend only within positive territory and never become bleak. Because these two sub-groups produce fundamentally different viewing experiences — one bleak, one bittersweet — they were separated into two types. The presence or absence of negative entry, rather than density alone, is the decisive boundary between Macbeth-type and Much Ado-type plays.

This yields six types in total: two descending forms separated by negative entry (Macbeth-type and Much Ado-type), one rising (As You Like It-type), one valley-shaped (Henry V-type), one inverted-arch (Othello-type), and one flat plateau (Love’s Labor’s Lost-type).

We considered whether fewer types (merging the two descending forms) or more types (splitting the rising group further) would better capture the data. Six was the configuration at which every type was defined by a coherent combination of emotional and network signatures, while remaining small enough for practical curation use. A small number of boundary cases — discussed where they occur — sit between types but do not undermine the overall structure.

Summary of Classification Variables

VariableSourceRole
Arc shapeLOWESS curve geometryPrimary split: descent / rise / valley / arch / flat
SlopeAct5 − Act1 VADER meanDirection and steepness of the arc
Negative entryAny act mean < 0Separates Macbeth-type (true descent) from Much Ado-type (cooling within positive range)
DensityEdges ÷ possible edgesTight ensemble vs. sparse world
Q ModularityLeiden separationUnified vs. fragmented cast
CommunitiesLeiden cluster countNumber of independent character groups
Avg Edge WeightMean co-occurrenceDepth of character interaction
03

The Six Types

Six Types at a Glance

The analysis produced six types, each named after its representative work. The cards below summarize all six; detailed profiles follow. Tap any card to jump to its profile.

Macbeth-type
Slow Burn + Fractured Factions
Crosses into negative sentiment; sparse, faction-split network
View profile →
Much Ado-type
Cooling Warmth + Tight Ensemble
Cools but stays positive; highest density and interaction weight
View profile →
As You Like It-type
Slow Build + Scattered World
Rising arc; lowest density, most communities
View profile →
Henry V-type
Mid-Act Valley + Resilient Core
Valley-shaped arc; mid density, relationships hold
View profile →
Othello-type
Inverted Arch + Claustrophobic Closeness
Peak-then-fall arc; dense, tightly bound cast
View profile →
Love’s Labor’s Lost-type
Sustained Tone + Light Network
Flat arc; wide reach, lowest interaction weight
View profile →
①

Macbeth-type

Keyword · Slow Burn + Fractured Factions
Representative Work: Macbeth8 plays

Emotional Flow

Begins in positive territory and descends progressively into negative values, ending below zero. The arc moves one direction — downward — without recovery. Darkness accumulates across acts rather than striking suddenly.

Character Network

Sparse structure (type avg Density 0.495) split into distinct factions with few cross-group ties. The thinness makes betrayal and isolation feel architecturally inevitable — relationships are too thin to absorb the weight the plot demands.

−0.19
Slope
0.290
Density
0.355
Q Modularity
4
Communities
1.63
Avg Weight

Works in This Type

Macbeth+
A general murders his king to seize the throne, then unravels under guilt and paranoia.Textbook descent: positive opening collapses into negative; sparse network (D=0.290) isolates Macbeth as his alliances fracture.
Romeo and Juliet+
Two young lovers from feuding families fall in love and die.Steepest slope in the type (−0.23). Warm opening curdles into tragedy; tight household clusters split along the feud line.
Titus Andronicus+
A Roman general and his enemies spiral into mutual revenge and atrocity.Continuous descent into the corpus’s darkest register; fragmented revenge factions.
Timon of Athens+
A generous nobleman is abandoned by friends and dies a misanthrope.Sharp downward arc; high community count (6) captures the social abandonment that drives the fall.
Troilus and Cressida+
Love and honour collapse amid the stalemate of the Trojan War.Bitter descent; only two communities but cynicism pervades both Greek and Trojan camps.
Cymbeline+
Mistaken jealousy and exile threaten a marriage before a late reconciliation.Net negative entry places it here despite romance elements; fragmented (Q=0.396) court-and-exile worlds.
Julius Caesar+
Conspirators assassinate Caesar; civil war consumes them.Gentle but steady descent; factional network (conspirators vs. loyalists) mirrors the political fracture.
Hamlet+
A prince feigns madness to avenge his murdered father; nearly everyone dies.Slope just negative; brooding arc never recovers. Three communities capture the court, the family, and the outsiders.

Target Viewers & Viewing Situations

Because this type pairs a downward emotional curve with a sparse, faction-split network, it suits viewers drawn to betrayal, mounting dread, and the collapse of relationships — those who want to feel catastrophe build and are comfortable with bleak endings. Late-evening, full-attention viewing.

Streaming Front“The Downward Spiral — Slow-Burning Tragedy”
②

Much Ado-type

Keyword · Cooling Warmth + Tight Ensemble
Representative Work: Much Ado About Nothing7 plays

Emotional Flow

Begins high on the positive register and descends across acts — but never enters negative territory. The warmth cools without collapsing; the resolution carries a slight sting rather than full triumph.

Character Network

The highest average density (0.585) and interaction weight (2.22) in the corpus. Characters are tightly bound and interact constantly — a unified cast with few internal boundaries. The warmth is structurally real; the network refuses to fall apart even as the mood cools.

−0.15
Slope
0.661
Density
0.222
Q Modularity
3
Communities
2.27
Avg Weight

Works in This Type

Much Ado About Nothing+
Two couples are tricked into and out of love before a double wedding.Warm arc that dips with the slander plot but stays positive; one of the densest casts (D=0.661) with constant cross-talk.
Twelfth Night+
Shipwreck, disguise, and mistaken identity tangle a love triangle.Highest density in the type (0.821); the tight ensemble cools gently from festive to wistful.
The Merry Wives of Windsor+
Two wives outwit the boastful Falstaff.Dense, gossipy community network (D=0.736); comedic warmth softens without ever darkening.
The Taming of the Shrew+
A volatile courtship ends in an uneasy marriage.Highest interaction weight in the corpus (2.86); warm-but-bitter aftertaste fits the cooling arc.
Measure for Measure+
A duke tests his deputy, exposing hypocrisy around mercy and law.A “problem comedy” — positive range but morally uneasy; cooling arc captures its ambivalence.
The Two Gentlemen of Verona+
Friendship and love collide as two friends pursue the same woman.Light, near-flat positive arc; unified cast (Q=0.036) with little fragmentation.
Antony and Cleopatra ✦ boundary+
The Roman general and Egyptian queen choose love over empire and fall.Boundary case — see note below.
✦ Boundary case — Antony and Cleopatra

Antony and Cleopatra is a boundary case. Its slope is technically positive (+0.02) and its network is unusually fragmented (8 communities), neither of which match this type’s typical signature. We classified it here on a stricter emotional criterion: although the slope is slightly positive, the play’s overall sentiment stays within the cooling positive-to-neutral band that defines Much Ado-type — it never rises in the sustained way that defines As You Like It-type, and never enters Macbeth-type darkness. The fragmented network reflects its sprawling geography (Rome vs. Egypt) rather than a fundamentally different emotional shape. Our classification rule in such cases is: emotional range and arc shape take priority over network anomalies.

Target Viewers & Viewing Situations

Because this type combines a gently cooling (but never bleak) curve with the densest, warmest relationship networks, it suits viewers who enjoy ensemble warmth and sharp wit but don’t need a flawless happy ending. Sunday-afternoon, relaxed viewing.

Streaming Front“Warm & Witty — Bittersweet Ensembles”
③

As You Like It-type

Keyword · Slow Build + Scattered World
Representative Work: As You Like It8 plays

Emotional Flow

Begins low or negative and ascends continuously to the final act. The work moves away from darkness, accumulating hope and resolution. The ending sits higher than the start.

Character Network

Lowest average density (0.368) and most communities (avg 4.4) in the corpus. Multiple independent groups operate across a wide world. The dispersion mirrors the narrative logic: scattered arcs that gradually converge as the emotional register rises.

+0.10
Slope
0.474
Density
0.253
Q Modularity
4
Communities
1.72
Avg Weight

Works in This Type

As You Like It+
Exiles find love and renewal in the Forest of Arden.Clear upward arc; scattered forest-and-court groups (4 communities) converge in the joyful finale.
The Tempest ✦ boundary+
A magician orchestrates a storm to reconcile old enemies.Boundary case — see note below.
Pericles+
A prince endures shipwreck and loss before reuniting with his family.Strong rise (+0.11); the most dispersed network in the corpus (7 communities) traces his scattered voyages.
The Winter’s Tale+
Jealousy destroys a family that is restored sixteen years later.Famous two-half structure: darkness then renewal; the rising arc captures the redemptive second half.
Henry IV, Part 2+
Prince Hal matures and ascends to the throne as Falstaff fades.Steep rise (+0.18) as Hal grows into kingship; many loosely linked political factions.
Henry VI, Part 1+
England’s fortunes in France amid the start of dynastic conflict.Steepest rise in the type (+0.21); sprawling, fragmented network of nobles and soldiers.
Henry VI, Part 3+
The Wars of the Roses reach their height.Upward arc (+0.07) with a highly fragmented dynastic network — one piece of the trilogy’s larger descent-and-recovery shape discussed in the Conclusion.
Coriolanus+
A war hero is exiled by the people he served, then turns on Rome.Slight positive slope (+0.02) but classified here on arc shape: the play moves from violent confrontation toward Coriolanus’s tragic recognition — a low-to-higher emotional trajectory across the highly fragmented (6 communities) political world.
✦ Boundary case — The Tempest

The Tempest is a boundary case. Its network is unusually dense (D=0.706) for this type, since the action is confined to one island. Following our classification rule, the strong, unbroken upward emotional trajectory across the whole play — from storm and grievance to reconciliation and freedom — takes priority over the network anomaly. The dense network reflects the single setting, not a different emotional shape.

Target Viewers & Viewing Situations

Because this type pairs a rising curve with a dispersed, multi-group network, it suits viewers who want momentum and redemption across a broad canvas — stories of growth and recovery. Good for morning or weekend viewing, or as a palate cleanser after heavier content.

Streaming Front“Against the Odds — Journeys of Renewal”
④

Henry V-type

Keyword · Mid-Act Valley + Resilient Core
Representative Work: Henry V7 plays

Emotional Flow

Dips to a low point in the middle acts, then recovers toward the end — a valley shape. The viewing experience carries the weight of a crisis and the release of its resolution.

Character Network

Mid-range density with meaningful interaction weight — the relationships hold together through the valley. The crisis forges rather than severs the central bonds, leaving a resilient core.

+0.04
Slope
0.222
Density
0.453
Q Modularity
4
Communities
1.57
Avg Weight

Works in This Type

Henry V+
A young king unites his men and wins against the odds at Agincourt.Classic valley: pre-battle dread sinks, victory lifts. The army’s bonds hold firm — the resilient core.
A Midsummer Night’s Dream+
Lovers and fairies tangle in the woods before all is set right.Mid-play confusion dips the mood; resolution restores it. Extremely dense cast (D=0.868) stays intact.
All’s Well That Ends Well+
A woman wins, loses, and finally secures the husband she chose.Down-then-up arc; relationships strained but ultimately mended.
The Merchant of Venice+
A merchant’s bond to a moneylender threatens his life before a clever rescue.Trial-scene crisis forms the valley; the friend-group core holds and recovers.
King Lear ✦ boundary+
A king divides his kingdom, is betrayed, and finds clarity too late.Boundary case — see note below.
Henry VI, Part 2+
Faction and rebellion erode royal authority.Mid-play breakdown of order; network holds enough structure to register as valley rather than free-fall.
King John+
A contested crown drives war, betrayal, and an uneasy settlement.Dips through political crisis then partly recovers; core alliances persist.
✦ Boundary case — King Lear

King Lear is a boundary case, and an important one. It is universally read as Shakespeare’s bleakest tragedy, yet its act-level curve bottoms out in the middle and recovers slightly at the end rather than descending all the way down like Macbeth. We classified it here on that emotional signature: the late scenes carry a fragile, hard-won clarity absent from a pure Macbeth-type descent. Its dense network (D=0.654) also matches the resilient-core pattern. For curation this distinction matters — a viewer ready for catharsis is not the same as one who wants unrelieved devastation.

Target Viewers & Viewing Situations

Because this type combines a down-then-up curve with a network that survives the crisis intact, it suits viewers who find tension satisfying when it resolves — those who want to feel the weight of a problem before the relief of its solution. Weekend evenings; good for group viewing.

Streaming Front“Through the Storm — Crisis & Catharsis”
⑤

Othello-type

Keyword · Inverted Arch + Claustrophobic Closeness
Representative Work: Othello4 plays

Emotional Flow

Reaches its emotional peak in the middle acts, then falls toward the end — an inverted arch. The highest moment is the beginning of the undoing, making the fall feel structurally prepared rather than accidental.

Character Network

Dense, unified, heavily interacting casts — structurally similar to the Much Ado-type, yet the arc runs opposite. The tight network is the dramatic irony: characters who are intensely close destroy one another precisely because of that closeness. Betrayal from within a claustrophobic space.

−0.12
Slope
0.669
Density
0.175
Q Modularity
3
Communities
2.49
Avg Weight

Works in This Type

Othello+
A general is manipulated into murdering his innocent wife, then himself.The defining inverted arch: marital bliss peaks early, then Iago’s poison drags it down. Dense cast (D=0.669) makes the betrayal claustrophobic.
Richard III+
A ruthless schemer murders his way to the crown, then is overthrown.Rises through cunning conquest, peaks at coronation, then falls. Tightly interlinked court of victims and accomplices.
Henry IV, Part 1+
Prince Hal balances tavern life and rebellion, triumphing at battle.Peak-then-settle arc; dense web linking court, rebels, and the Falstaff circle.
The Comedy of Errors+
Two sets of separated twins cause escalating confusion in one city.Farce builds to a peak of chaos before resolving; very dense (D=0.728), claustrophobic single-city setting.

Target Viewers & Viewing Situations

Because this type sets a peak-then-fall curve inside an unusually dense, closed network, it suits viewers who want a powerful reversal and the tension of intimacy turned dangerous — fans of dramatic irony and betrayal from within. Best as a single, uninterrupted session.

Streaming Front“Too High to Fall — Intimate Betrayals”
⑥

Love’s Labor’s Lost-type

Keyword · Sustained Tone + Light Network
Representative Work: Love’s Labor’s Lost3 plays

Emotional Flow

Minimal movement across acts. VADER scores stay stable — neither ascending nor descending much. A plateau: consistent in register, without the tension of ascent or weight of descent. These works sustain a tone rather than develop one.

Character Network

Wide reach but the lowest average interaction weight in the corpus (1.54). Characters are connected but interact less deeply. The structural lightness echoes the content: these plays privilege rhetoric and wit over plot and relationship transformation.

+0.02
Slope
0.843
Density
0.023
Q Modularity
3
Communities
1.76
Avg Weight

Works in This Type

Love’s Labor’s Lost+
Four men forswear women to study, then immediately fall in love.Flat, sustained witty register; the play is built on wordplay and rhetoric, mirrored by the light interaction weight.
Richard II+
A king is deposed in a war of words and ceremony more than action.Famously language-driven; near-zero slope. Power shifts through speech, not battle — the lightest of networks.
Henry VIII+
Court intrigue and royal divorce told through ceremony and rhetoric.Stately, even register; processional and oratorical rather than dynamic — lowest interaction weight in the type.

Target Viewers & Viewing Situations

Because this type holds a steady emotional register over a wide but lightly connected network, it suits viewers who come for language — wordplay, oratory, formal rhetoric — rather than emotional swings. Daytime or focused, text-in-hand viewing.

Streaming Front“Words Over Action — Rhetoric & Wit”
04

Conclusion

Summary and Significance

This project set out to test whether Shakespeare’s 37 plays could be classified for streaming curation using only two measurable dimensions — emotional trajectory and character network structure — without relying on the traditional comedy / tragedy / history labels. The answer is yes: six internally consistent types emerged from the data, each named after a representative work, each defined by a coherent combination of emotional arc and network signature, and each producing a distinct, recognizable viewing experience.

The methodological contribution is a classification framework that is reproducible, data-grounded, and decoupled from genre tradition. Any new work — Shakespearean or otherwise — can be scored on the same two dimensions and assigned to a type or flagged as a boundary case. The framework converts a curation problem (which usually relies on tags written by humans) into a measurement problem (which can be applied at scale).

The substantive contribution is the demonstration that these data-driven types cut across traditional genre lines in ways that better predict how a work feels to watch. The Henry VI history trilogy splits across two types — Parts 1 and 3 in the rising As You Like It-type, Part 2 in the valley-shaped Henry V-type — revealing that the trilogy as a whole enacts a single descent-and-recovery shape that genre flattens. Othello shares its network architecture with the comedies, and that shared closeness is precisely what makes its betrayal devastating. The flat, language-driven Love’s Labor’s Lost-type unites a comedy and two histories that genre would never group together.

For the streaming platform, this translates directly into curation logic. A viewer who finishes one Macbeth-type work can be reliably recommended another, regardless of whether tradition calls it a tragedy or a history. The six types map cleanly onto familiar front-end categories — prestige tragedy, bittersweet ensemble, journey of renewal, crisis-and-catharsis, intimate betrayal, and rhetorical wit — and the same two-dimensional method can be extended to the platform’s wider film and series library. That extension is the natural next step beyond this report.

Limitations and Future Directions

Four constraints shape how this system should be applied, and each suggests a direction for future refinement.

Limitation 1

Sentiment tool calibration

VADER was trained on modern English and may misread Early Modern verse — archaic negation, sustained irony, and metaphor can be scored incorrectly. LOWESS smoothing reduces noise but cannot correct systematic lexical bias. A sentiment model fine-tuned on period texts would improve accuracy and may resolve some boundary cases.

Limitation 2

Network signal is structural, not qualitative

Speech co-occurrence captures that two characters share a scene, not the nature of their interaction. Two characters in bitter conflict and two in deep affection register identically if they co-occur equally often. Adding sentiment-weighted edges (positive vs. negative co-occurrence) or directional edges (who addresses whom) would capture relationship quality, not just quantity, and might distinguish the Much Ado-type from the Othello-type using network data alone.

Limitation 3

Boundary cases and the need for confidence scoring

Antony and Cleopatra, The Tempest, and King Lear each fit their assigned type on one dimension while diverging on the other. We resolved these by giving the emotional arc priority in our classification hierarchy, but a production-grade recommender would benefit from confidence scores or multi-tag assignments — allowing a single title to belong primarily to one type and secondarily to another, rather than being forced into one category.

Limitation 4

Text, not performance

The analysis reflects the original English scripts. Translations, heavy cuts, or reinterpretive stagings can shift both the emotional arc and the network. Future work could measure how much these classifications hold across specific production versions, which matters for a platform that licenses adaptations rather than texts.

These limitations define the agenda for the next stage of the project: refining the sentiment model, enriching the network signal with edge qualities, building a confidence-scoring layer for boundary cases, and validating the framework against specific film and television adaptations. The classification system presented in this report is a working foundation — small enough to deploy, principled enough to extend.

05
